1:
On Exodus 16:1 — “And they took their journey from Elim… and came unto the wilderness of Sin… on the fifteenth day of the second month after their departing out of the land of Egypt” — the witnesses mark the arrival at the wilderness of Sin.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the timing. Adam Clarke: from Elim they came to the wilderness of Sin, where they murmured for bread. Ellicott: Sin was reached exactly one month after the departure from Egypt. The Puritan Henry and Wesley: a month’s provision, brought out of Egypt on the fifteenth of the first month, was spent by the fifteenth of the second.

The verse fixes the place and time. One month out of Egypt, their provisions spent, Israel reaches the wilderness of Sin. The stage is set for the manna.

So the witnesses see Israel come, exactly a month after the Exodus, to the wilderness where God would feed them from heaven. The witnesses note the careful dating: “on the fifteenth day of the second month” — Ellicott observes that the wilderness of Sin was reached precisely one month after the departure from Egypt, which had been on the fifteenth of the first month. They mark the practical occasion the expositors draw out: Henry and Wesley reckon that the host of Israel had carried a month’s provision out of Egypt, and that by this day it was all spent; the need that prompts the murmuring and the miracle is thus exactly accounted for. They observe the journey from Elim, the place of the twelve wells and seventy palms, into the barren tract of Sin, where no food was to be had. They note the providence in the timing: God let their own store run out before he supplied his own, that they might learn their dependence on him and not on what they brought from Egypt. They observe that the wilderness, where nothing grew, was the very place God chose to spread a table; the barrenness of the desert magnifies the bounty of the gift to come. They note that the stage is now set: a great multitude, their bread gone, in a place that could not feed them. The verse thus fixes the place and time: the wilderness of Sin, a month out of Egypt, provisions spent — the occasion, in the next verse, of their murmuring.
2:
On Exodus 16:2 — “And the whole congregation of the children of Israel murmured against Moses and Aaron in the wilderness” — the witnesses mark the third murmuring.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the rebellion. Adam Clarke: a further proof of the degraded state of the people’s minds. Ellicott: this is the third murmuring — the first at Pi-hahiroth, the second at Marah (Exodus 15:24), the third here. The Reformed Calvin: not some only, but all gathered into mobs, murmuring with one consent. Wesley: against Moses and Aaron, God’s viceregents.

The verse records the complaint. The whole congregation murmurs against Moses and Aaron for want of bread. Their discontent rises again.

So the witnesses see Israel, their food gone, break out once more into murmuring against their leaders. The witnesses note the breadth of the rebellion: “the whole congregation… murmured” — Calvin observes that Moses does not say some only complained, but that they were all gathered into mobs, as in a conspiracy, murmuring with one consent (though, he adds, the universal term may admit some exception). They mark the count Ellicott keeps: this is the third murmuring of Israel since the Exodus — the first at the Red Sea when Pharaoh’s host appeared, the second at Marah over the bitter water (Exodus 15:24), and now the third for want of bread; a settled habit of discontent is forming. They observe the target: “against Moses and Aaron” — Wesley notes these were God’s viceregents, so that to murmur against them was in truth to murmur against God who sent them. They note Clarke’s severe judgment, that this repeated murmuring betrays the degraded, carnal state of minds long beaten down by slavery, quick to forget the wonders already wrought. They observe the ingratitude: so soon after the Red Sea and Marah, the people doubt and complain rather than trust the God who had so plainly cared for them. They note that real need is no excuse for the unbelief that murmurs instead of praying. The verse thus records the complaint: the whole congregation murmuring — whose extravagant words the next verse reports.
3:
On Exodus 16:3 — “Would to God we had died by the hand of the LORD in the land of Egypt, when we sat by the flesh pots… for ye have brought us forth into this wilderness, to kill this whole assembly with hunger” — the witnesses mark the extravagance of their complaint.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the exaggeration. Adam Clarke: the “flesh pots” — the boilers where their taskmasters fed the slaves; they remembered the food, not the bondage. Ellicott: the Hebrew has no “God” — “would that we had died”; perhaps an allusion to the plagues. Wesley: they so undervalue their deliverance that they wish they had died in Egypt by the plagues.

The verse gives their wild words. They wish they had died in Egypt amid the flesh pots, charging Moses with bringing them out to starve. Their unbelief grows reckless.

So the witnesses see the murmuring break into reckless and ungrateful speech. The witnesses note the wish: “would to God we had died… in the land of Egypt” — Ellicott observes that the Hebrew contains no name of God, reading simply “would that we had died,” and that there may be an allusion to the plagues, as if they wished they had perished with the Egyptians rather than be brought to this. They mark the false memory of Egypt: “when we sat by the flesh pots, and when we did eat bread to the full” — Clarke explains the “flesh pots” as the great boilers where the taskmasters cooked food for their gangs of slaves; the people remember the fare but forget the whips, painting their bondage as a time of plenty. They observe the cruel charge: “ye have brought us forth into this wilderness, to kill this whole assembly with hunger” — they accuse Moses and Aaron of leading them out on purpose to starve them, a monstrous slander on their deliverers and on God. They note Wesley’s point that they so undervalued their deliverance as to wish themselves dead in the house of bondage. They observe the irrationality of unbelief: present want blots out the memory of past mercies and the promise of future ones; the table at the Red Sea is forgotten in the hunger of a day. They note that discontent magnifies the comforts of the old life and forgets its miseries. The verse thus gives their wild words: a wish to have died in Egypt — which God answers, in the next verse, with bread from heaven.
4:
On Exodus 16:4 — “Then said the LORD unto Moses, Behold, I will rain bread from heaven for you; and the people shall go out and gather a certain rate every day, that I may prove them, whether they will walk in my law, or no” — the witnesses mark the promise of bread from heaven.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the supernatural gift. Adam Clarke: not a production of the desert, else they had had it before. Ellicott: “bread from heaven” a celestial, divine supply, not mere food out of the air. The Reformed Calvin: God’s extraordinary kindness to a depraved people, yet with a test. Wesley: Israel, typifying the church of the firstborn, received their bread from heaven (Psalm 104:14).

The verse gives the promise and the test. God will rain bread from heaven, gathered daily, to prove their obedience. Mercy and trial go together.

So the witnesses see God answer the murmuring not with rebuke but with the promise of bread rained down from heaven. The witnesses note the marvel announced: “I will rain bread from heaven for you” — the expositors insist this was no natural product of the desert (Clarke: else they would have had it before), but a supernatural, celestial supply; Ellicott calls it a divine giving of their daily food, “bread from heaven” in the fullest sense. They mark the manner: “the people shall go out and gather a certain rate every day” — a daily portion, to be gathered fresh each morning, that they might live in continual dependence on God. They observe the test attached: “that I may prove them, whether they will walk in my law, or no” — the daily gathering, with its rules, was a trial of obedience and trust; would they take only their portion, leave none till morning, and keep the Sabbath as commanded? They note Calvin’s wonder at God’s kindness, feeding so depraved and ungrateful a people, yet joining a test that their hearts might be revealed. They observe the typology the witnesses draw, especially Wesley: Israel, a figure of the church of the firstborn written in heaven, fittingly received their bread from heaven; and the manna prefigures Christ, the true Bread from heaven (John 6:31), and teaches the daily prayer for daily bread (Matthew 6:11). They note that the gift was also a discipline. The verse thus gives the promise and test: bread from heaven, gathered daily, to prove them — the sixth-day provision specified in the next verse.
5:
On Exodus 16:5 — “And it shall come to pass, that on the sixth day they shall prepare that which they bring in; and it shall be twice as much as they gather daily” — the witnesses mark the double portion for the sixth day.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: the sixth day after the first giving of the manna; on it a double portion was provided against the Sabbath. The Reformed Gill: the sixth day of the week, reckoned from the first raining of the bread. Calvin: because the Sabbath is mentioned, the law of rest is in view; God made trial whether they would keep it.

The verse foretells the sixth-day gift. On that day they gather twice as much, against the day of rest. The Sabbath is provided for beforehand.

So the witnesses see God make provision, before ever the manna fell, for the keeping of the Sabbath. The witnesses note the special direction: “on the sixth day… it shall be twice as much as they gather daily” — on the sixth day of the week a double portion of manna would be given and gathered, that there might be food for the seventh day, on which none would fall. They mark the careful reckoning Ellicott and Gill observe: the “sixth day” is counted from the first giving of the manna, falling out as the sixth of the week. They observe the purpose, which Calvin draws out: since the Sabbath is here brought in, the law of holy rest is already in view; God by this arrangement made trial whether the people would honour the day of rest, gathering double beforehand and resting on the seventh. They note the wonder of the provision: God so ordered the heavenly bread that it fell six days and not the seventh, and that the sixth day’s double portion would keep, while on other days the surplus bred worms — a standing weekly miracle testifying to the Sabbath. They observe that the rest was so honoured by God himself that he would not send the manna upon it, but fed his people from the day before. They note that the Sabbath is here treated as already known, woven into the very giving of the bread. They observe that God provides for the keeping of his own ordinance. The verse thus foretells the sixth-day gift: a double portion against the Sabbath — which Moses and Aaron, in the next verses, announce to the people.
6:
On Exodus 16:6 — “And Moses and Aaron said unto all the children of Israel, At even, then ye shall know that the LORD hath brought you out from the land of Egypt” — the witnesses mark the promise that they shall know the LORD.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the rebuke implied. Adam Clarke: after all the miracles, they still supposed Moses and Aaron had brought them out; now they shall know it was the Lord. Ellicott: “at even” alludes to the quails, which came that evening. The Reformed Calvin: this answers their wicked taunt that they had been betrayed by Moses and Aaron.

The verse promises proof. By evening they shall know the LORD brought them out of Egypt. The gift will vindicate God’s leading.

So the witnesses see Moses and Aaron turn the people’s complaint back upon them, promising that the coming gift will prove whose work the Exodus was. The witnesses note the words: “at even, then ye shall know that the LORD hath brought you out” — by the evening, when the quails would come, the people would have proof that it was the LORD, not merely Moses and Aaron, who had led them from Egypt. They mark the rebuke implied, which Clarke draws out: after all the miracles already wrought, the people still spoke as though their deliverance were the work of Moses and Aaron, men who had brought them into trouble; God would now show them, by feeding them, that he himself was their deliverer and provider. They observe Calvin’s point that this directly answers the wicked taunt of verse 3, in which they complained of being betrayed by their leaders into the wilderness; God vindicates his servants by owning the deliverance as his own. They note Ellicott’s observation that “at even” points to the quails that covered the camp that evening, the first half of the double gift. They observe the grace in this: God answers their slander not with destruction but with food, and uses the very meeting of their need to teach them who he is. They note that the gift was to be a revelation: in being fed, they would know the LORD. The verse thus promises proof: by evening they shall know the LORD brought them out — and, in the morning, the next verse adds, see his glory.
7:
On Exodus 16:7 — “And in the morning, then ye shall see the glory of the LORD; for that he heareth your murmurings against the LORD: and what are we, that ye murmur against us?” — the witnesses mark the glory of the LORD and the murmuring heard.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the glory. Adam Clarke: the glory of the Lord here seems distinct from the Lord, for it “heareth” their murmurings; the Lord may be put for himself. Ellicott: the reference is to the manna lying round the host in the morning, not the cloud-appearance of Exodus 16:10. The Reformed Gill: the glory shown in raining bread about their tents.

The verse promises the morning glory. In the morning they shall see God’s glory, for he has heard their murmurings. The complaint reaches God himself.

So the witnesses see the promise enlarged: not only food but a sight of God’s glory, and a reminder that their murmurings were heard in heaven. The witnesses note the morning gift: “in the morning, then ye shall see the glory of the LORD” — Ellicott and Gill take this chiefly of the manna lying round about the host at dawn, the glory of God displayed in raining bread from heaven about their tents (distinct, Ellicott notes, from the appearing of the glory in the cloud at Exodus 16:10). They mark the solemn word about their complaint: “for that he heareth your murmurings against the LORD” — their grumbling, though spoken against Moses and Aaron, was in truth against the LORD, and he had heard it all. They observe the disclaimer of the leaders: “what are we, that ye murmur against us?” — Moses and Aaron put away the people’s anger from themselves, owning they were but instruments; to murmur against them was to murmur against God who sent them. They note Clarke’s reflection on the phrase “the glory of the Lord,” which seems here almost distinct from the Lord, since it is said to hear their murmurings — though the term may simply stand for God himself in his manifested presence. They observe the double lesson: the people would see God’s glory in his bounty and feel his rebuke in his hearing; mercy and reproof in one. They note that nothing murmured is hidden from God. The verse thus promises the morning glory: God’s glory seen, their murmurings heard — the charge pressed home in the next verse.
8:
On Exodus 16:8 — “And Moses said… the LORD shall give you in the evening flesh to eat, and in the morning bread to the full… your murmurings are not against us, but against the LORD” — the witnesses mark the double gift and the true object of the murmuring.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: in the evening flesh (the quails), in the morning bread to the full (the manna); “what are we?” — only his servants, obeying his commands; your murmurings are against the Lord. The Reformed Gill notes Moses confirming the promise.

The verse names the gift and the offence. Flesh at evening, bread in the morning, from the LORD, against whom their murmurings truly are. God hears and yet provides.

So the witnesses see Moses spell out the coming provision and press home against whom the people’s complaint really lay. The witnesses note the double gift specified: “in the evening flesh to eat, and in the morning bread to the full” — Clarke identifies the evening flesh as the quails and the morning bread as the manna, given in such plenty that they should eat to the full; God would meet both the craving for meat and the need of bread. They mark the repeated insistence on the source: it is “the LORD” who gives, not Moses; the leaders are but the channel. They observe the disclaimer pressed again: “what are we?” — Clarke renders the sense, only his servants, obeying his commands; Moses and Aaron will not stand between God and the people as the object either of their thanks or their blame. They observe the solemn conclusion: “your murmurings are not against us, but against the LORD” — the people must understand that their discontent strikes past the visible leaders to God himself, who had appointed their way; to murmur at his providence is to quarrel with him. They note the grace that, even while charging home the sin, God still promises the food; he answers the murmuring with mercy and rebuke together. They observe that the gift would prove the giver. They note the lesson that complaint against God’s ordering of our lot is complaint against God. The verse thus names the gift and the offence: flesh and bread from the LORD, murmured against — the congregation summoned, in the next verse, before him.
9:
On Exodus 16:9 — “And Moses spake unto Aaron, Say unto all the congregation… Come near before the LORD: for he hath heard your murmurings” — the witnesses mark the summons before the LORD.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: “come near before the Lord” — to some place where the Lord manifested his presence, perhaps a tent of meeting before the great tabernacle was built. The Reformed Calvin: he cites them as criminals before God’s tribunal, alluding to the cloud, the visible token of God’s presence.

The verse summons the people. They are called near before the LORD, who has heard their murmurings. They are arraigned before his presence.

So the witnesses see the congregation summoned to draw near before the LORD, who had heard their complaint. The witnesses note the call, conveyed as ever through Aaron the spokesman: “Come near before the LORD: for he hath heard your murmurings” — the people are bidden to present themselves before God’s manifested presence. They mark the place the expositors suppose: Clarke conjectures some appointed spot where the Lord made known his presence — perhaps an early tent of meeting, before the great tabernacle was reared — though above all it points to the pillar of cloud, the visible sign of God among them. They observe Calvin’s reading of the summons as a kind of arraignment: Moses cites the murmurers as criminals before the tribunal of God, that they may learn their grumbling was not unobserved but heard and judged; they stand before the very One they had offended. They note the awe of it: a murmuring people called to stand before the glory they had provoked, to receive both the rebuke of their sin and the proof of God’s care. They observe that God deals with them openly and directly, not through rumour but face to face with his presence. They note the solemnity that their complaint is treated as a matter to be answered before God himself. They observe that the summons prepares for the appearing of the glory. The verse thus summons the people: come near before the LORD who has heard — at whose word, in the next verse, the glory appears in the cloud.
10:
On Exodus 16:10 — “And it came to pass, as Aaron spake unto the whole congregation… they looked toward the wilderness, and, behold, the glory of the LORD appeared in the cloud” — the witnesses mark the appearing of the glory.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the manifestation. Adam Clarke: Aaron now became the spokesman, the minister of Moses to the Hebrews (Exodus 7:1). Ellicott: the “cloud” is the pillar of cloud; it was before this they had been bidden to appear. Wesley: an extraordinary and sudden brightness.

The verse shows the glory. As Aaron speaks, the glory of the LORD appears in the cloud. God answers the summons with his presence.

So the witnesses see God confirm the summons by a sudden, awful manifestation of his glory. The witnesses note the timing: “as Aaron spake unto the whole congregation” — even while the word was being delivered, before he had well finished, the glory broke forth; God set his seal on his servant’s message at once. They mark the act of the people: “they looked toward the wilderness” — turning toward the open desert, away from the camp, where the cloud stood. They observe the manifestation: “the glory of the LORD appeared in the cloud” — Ellicott takes the cloud to be the pillar that led them, in or upon which the divine glory now shone forth; Wesley describes it as an extraordinary and sudden brightness, a blaze of light bursting from the cloud. They note Clarke’s observation that Aaron here acts as the spokesman and minister of Moses to the people, as he had earlier been to Pharaoh (Exodus 7:1). They observe the purpose of the appearing: God shows his glory both to awe the murmurers and to authenticate the promise of food about to be fulfilled; the same presence that rebuked their sin would shortly feed them. They note that the visible glory turned the people’s complaint into reverence, confronting them with the God they had provoked. They observe that God answers being murmured against by drawing near. The verse thus shows the glory: appearing in the cloud as Aaron spoke — from which, in the next verses, God speaks the promise of flesh and bread.
11:
On Exodus 16:11 — “And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying” — the witnesses mark the LORD speaking from the cloud.

The grammatical-historical Gill: the LORD spake out of the bright and glorious cloud. The Reformed Calvin: Moses shows he did nothing without God’s command, but faithfully discharged the office of a minister; unless he had spoken according to God’s word, he would have been rash in promising.

The verse opens the divine word. The LORD speaks to Moses from the glory. The promise is grounded in God’s own command.

So the witnesses see God himself speak from the glory to confirm what Moses had promised. The witnesses note the source of the word: Gill marks that the LORD spake “out of the bright and glorious cloud” that had just appeared — the manifested presence now utters its message. They observe the function of the verse, brief as it is: it shows that the great promise of flesh and bread, which Moses and Aaron had already announced to the people, rested not on their own word but on God’s express command. They mark Calvin’s observation here, that Moses by this is shown to have done nothing of himself, but to have faithfully and modestly discharged the office of a minister; and that, unless he had spoken according to God’s word, he would have been rash and presumptuous in promising what was not in his power to give. They note the order of the narrative: Moses had spoken the promise (vv. 6–8) before the LORD here formally gives it, because, as a true minister, he spoke beforehand only what he knew God would do; the divine word now ratifies the human announcement. They observe the comfort in this for God’s servants: they may speak boldly what God has commanded, sure that he will make it good. They note that the glory and the voice together authenticate the message. They observe that all rests on God’s speaking. The verse thus opens the divine word: the LORD speaks to Moses from the cloud — the content of which, the promise of meat and bread, the next verse gives.
12:
On Exodus 16:12 — “I have heard the murmurings of the children of Israel… At even ye shall eat flesh, and in the morning ye shall be filled with bread; and ye shall know that I am the LORD your God” — the witnesses mark the promise confirmed and its end.

The grammatical-historical Gill: God confirms what Moses and Aaron had said, that he had heard and disapproved their murmurings. Wesley: this gave proof of his power as the LORD and his favour as their God; when he plagued Egypt it was to make them know he is the LORD, when he provided for Israel it was that they should know he is their God.

The verse confirms the gift. God has heard; flesh at evening, bread in the morning, that they may know he is their God. The provision reveals the giver.

So the witnesses see God himself ratify the promise, naming both the gift and its gracious purpose. The witnesses note the acknowledgment: “I have heard the murmurings of the children of Israel” — Gill observes that God confirms what Moses and Aaron had said, owning that he had taken notice of the people’s grumbling and was displeased with it, yet would answer it with food. They mark the double gift renewed: “at even ye shall eat flesh, and in the morning ye shall be filled with bread” — the quails by evening, the manna by morning, given to the full. They observe the end God sets upon it: “ye shall know that I am the LORD your God” — Wesley draws out the rich sense of this, that the provision was to prove both his power as “the LORD” and his special favour as “your God”; for when God plagued the Egyptians it was that they might know he is the LORD, but when he fed the Israelites it was that they might know he is their God, bound to them in covenant love. They note the patience of God, who answers murmuring not with the plagues he sent on Egypt but with the bread of heaven, that his goodness might lead them to know him. They observe that the gift was a sermon: every meal in the wilderness preached the covenant God who fed them. They note that to be filled was to be taught. The verse thus confirms the gift: flesh and bread, that they may know their God — the quails coming, in the next verse, at evening.
13:
On Exodus 16:13 — “And it came to pass, that at even the quails came up, and covered the camp: and in the morning the dew lay round about the host” — the witnesses mark the quails and the dew.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the birds. Adam Clarke: the quail (selav), from a root “to be quiet,” great numbers coming to Egypt and Arabia. Ellicott: the common quail, which migrates in immense masses. The Puritan Henry: a feast of feathered fowl (Psalm 78:27), not locusts. Wesley: so tame they might take as many as they pleased.

The verse records the first gifts. Quails cover the camp at evening; dew lies round in the morning. The promise begins to be fulfilled.

So the witnesses see the promise made good: the quails at evening and, with the morning dew, the coming of the manna. The witnesses note the evening gift: “at even the quails came up, and covered the camp” — great flights of quail, the expositors explain, migratory birds that pass in immense masses over Egypt and Arabia, settled upon the camp; Henry identifies them as the “feathered fowl” of the Psalm (Psalm 78:27), and rejects the notion that locusts were meant. They mark Wesley’s observation that the birds were so tame, or so wearied with long flight, that the people might take as many as they pleased; the meat was given without labour. They observe Clarke’s note on the name selav, from a root meaning to be quiet or at ease, the quail known for living securely among the corn. They note the morning gift announced: “in the morning the dew lay round about the host” — the dew that fell first, with which (the next verse shows) the manna came. They observe the bounty and the order: flesh in the evening, bread in the morning, exactly as promised; God is punctual to his word. They note that the quails, given once here as a gracious supply, would later be given in wrath when the people lusted for flesh (Numbers 11:31); here it is mercy, there judgment. They observe that the wilderness is spread with God’s table. The verse thus records the first gifts: quails at evening, dew in the morning — and with the dew, the next verse shows, the manna.
14:
On Exodus 16:14 — “And when the dew that lay was gone up, behold, upon the face of the wilderness there lay a small round thing, as small as the hoar frost on the ground” — the witnesses mark the appearance of the manna.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the form. Keil and Delitzsch: a small round thing, fine as hoar frost. Adam Clarke: it fell with the dew, or rather the dew fell first and this upon it. Ellicott: what the manna was has been much disputed, two natural substances being compared, yet the miracle is plain. The Reformed Calvin: like dew condensed into small round grains, no natural product but miraculous.

The verse describes the manna. When the dew lifted, a small round thing like hoar frost lay on the ground. The bread of heaven appears.

So the witnesses see the manna first appear, a strange small substance left upon the ground when the dew was gone. The witnesses note the manner: “when the dew that lay was gone up” — the morning dew, drawn up by the sun, left behind it the manna; Clarke notes that the dew fell first and the manna upon or with it, the dew (some suppose) cooling and moistening the ground to receive it. They mark the appearance: “a small round thing, as small as the hoar frost on the ground” — fine, round grains scattered like frost over the face of the wilderness. They observe the long dispute the expositors record over what the manna was: Ellicott notes that two natural substances of the region have been compared with it, but that neither answers to the manna of Scripture, which fell in such quantity, kept one day and not the next, doubled on the sixth day and failed on the seventh; the miracle is not to be explained away. They note Calvin’s judgment that, though it was like dew condensed into small round grains, its abundance and properties show it was no natural product but a thing miraculously created for the people. They observe that God made the bread of heaven in a lowly form, small and frost-like, that the great gift came in a humble guise. They note the wonder that a whole nation was fed from grains like frost. They observe that the manna, type of Christ, came down quietly with the dew. The verse thus describes the manna: small, round, like hoar frost — which the people, in the next verse, do not know how to name.
15:
On Exodus 16:15 — “And when the children of Israel saw it, they said one to another, It is manna: for they wist not what it was. And Moses said unto them, This is the bread which the LORD hath given you to eat” — the witnesses mark the naming of the manna, with a disputed translation.

The grammatical-historical commentators dispute the rendering. Adam Clarke: a most unfortunate translation; the Hebrew man hu literally means “What is this?” Ellicott: certainly wrong as “It is manna”; either “What is this?” (LXX, Vulgate) or “This is a gift.” The Reformed Calvin: from a root meaning a prepared thing or an assigned portion. Wesley: “What is this? Manna descended from the clouds.”

The verse gives the name. Seeing the strange food, they ask “What is this?”; Moses calls it the bread the LORD has given. The wonder is named by a question.

So the witnesses see the people name the manna by their very question, not knowing what it was. The witnesses note the difficulty in the translation, on which the expositors are agreed. The Authorized Version’s “It is manna: for they wist not what it was” is, Clarke says bluntly, a most unfortunate rendering that contradicts itself — for if they called it manna, they knew its name. The Hebrew man hu, he and Ellicott explain, means either “What is this?” (so the Septuagint and Vulgate) or, as some render, “This is a gift”; the name “manna” arose from the question. They mark the sense: seeing the strange frost-like substance and not knowing what it was, the people asked one another, “What is this?” — and the food took its name from their wondering inquiry. They observe Moses’ answer: “This is the bread which the LORD hath given you to eat” — the unknown thing is explained as God’s gift, the promised bread from heaven. They note the typology the witnesses and the New Testament draw: this bread the LORD gave is a figure of Christ, the true Bread from heaven, of whom the world still asks “What is this?” (John 6:31). They observe that the gift was so far above nature that it had no name, and was called simply “What is it?” They note that God’s best gifts may at first be a mystery to those who receive them. The verse thus gives the name: “What is this?”, the bread the LORD has given — the rule for gathering it following in the next verse.
16:
On Exodus 16:16 — “This is the thing which the LORD hath commanded, Gather of it every man according to his eating, an omer for every man… according to the number of your persons” — the witnesses mark the rule of gathering.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the measure. Adam Clarke: the omer, from a root “to press or collect,” a measure of capacity. Ellicott: each man to gather according to his immediate need and his family’s; none to accumulate a store; an omer about three pints. The Reformed Calvin: a daily measure prescribed, with the Sabbath exception coming later.

The verse sets the rule. Each is to gather an omer a head, by his eating, for his household. The portion is measured to need.

So the witnesses see the gathering of the manna governed by a fixed and equal rule. The witnesses note the command: “gather of it every man according to his eating, an omer for every man” — each was to gather a set measure, an omer (about three English pints, Ellicott reckons), for every person in his tent, reckoned by the number of souls he had to feed. They mark the principle Ellicott draws out: each man was to gather according to his immediate need and that of his family, and no one was to seek to lay up a store; the supply was for the day, not for hoarding. They observe Clarke’s note on the “omer,” a measure of capacity named from a root meaning to press or collect together. They note Calvin’s observation that here the daily measure is prescribed, the exception for the Sabbath (the double portion that keeps) coming later in the chapter. They observe the equity of the rule: a fixed portion a head, neither more for the greedy nor less for the weak; the gathering was to match the eating, that none want and none waste. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: God measures his provision to his people’s need, giving enough and not to excess, and forbids the covetous laying-up that distrusts tomorrow’s supply. They observe that the rule trained them in daily dependence and in contentment with their portion. The verse thus sets the rule: an omer a head, by every man’s eating — which Israel, in the next verse, sets about to keep.
17:
On Exodus 16:17 — “And the children of Israel did so, and gathered, some more, some less” — the witnesses mark the gathering, more and less.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Clarke: they gathered according to their respective families, some more, some less, by the number of persons they had to supply. The Reformed Gill draws the type: believers going out of the world, leaving all behind for Christ, to gather the bread of life. Calvin notes that not all obeyed perfectly, some gathering more than was allowed.

The verse records the gathering. They go out and gather, some more, some less. The portions differ by household.

So the witnesses see Israel obey the command, going out to gather the manna in differing amounts. The witnesses note the obedience: “the children of Israel did so” — they went out in the morning, when the manna lay, and gathered it as Moses had directed. They mark the difference in quantity: “some more, some less” — Clarke and Keil explain this by the differing size of households, each gathering in proportion to the number of persons he had to feed, the able for the infirm, the head for his family. They observe Gill’s spiritual reading of the going-out to gather: a figure of believers leaving the world and all behind them for Christ, going out to gather the bread of life that he gives. They note Calvin’s sober qualification, that the obedience here was not perfect or greatly to be praised, since (as the chapter soon shows) some gathered more than was allowed and others broke the rule; the “more and less” was within God’s appointment, but human greed would presently exceed it. They observe that the gathering itself was a daily labour appointed by God: the bread was given freely from heaven, yet they must go out and gather it, the gift and the duty joined. They note that diligence was required even for a gift; none could lie still and be fed. They observe that the differing amounts set up the wonder of the next verse. The verse thus records the gathering: some more, some less — yet, the next verse shows, all alike supplied.
18:
On Exodus 16:18 — “And when they did mete it with an omer, he that gathered much had nothing over, and he that gathered little had no lack; they gathered every man according to his eating” — the witnesses mark the equal sufficiency of the manna.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the equalizing. Adam Clarke: he that gathered much had nothing over because his gathering was in proportion to his household. Ellicott: each gathered what he supposed about an omer a head; some over, some under, yet when measured each had just an omer apiece. Keil and Delitzsch trace the wonder.

The verse records the equality. Measured out, the much was not too much, the little not too little; each had an omer a head. None had excess, none want.

So the witnesses see the manna prove, in the measuring, to be exactly sufficient for every household, whatever the amount gathered. The witnesses note the result: “he that gathered much had nothing over, and he that gathered little had no lack” — when the heaps were measured by the omer, each household was found to have just an omer for every person, neither surplus nor shortfall. They mark the two ways the expositors account for it. Clarke and Ellicott note the providential proportion: each had gathered, knowingly or not, according to the number he had to feed, the larger families more and the smaller less, so that all came out even; some had over-estimated and some under, yet on measuring, each had exactly his omer apiece. Keil and Delitzsch and the others see in it the hand of God, who so ordered the gathering that none lacked and none abounded. They observe the lesson the witnesses draw, and which the apostle drew after them: that there should be an equality, the abundance of some supplying the want of others, so that he who gathers much has nothing over and he who gathers little no lack (2 Corinthians 8:15); the manna teaches the sharing of God’s bounty. They note that God’s provision exactly fits his people’s need: enough for all, excess for none. They observe that the daily bread was daily sufficient, and no more was required. The verse thus records the equality: the much not over, the little not lacking — the rule against keeping any till morning following in the next verse.
19:
On Exodus 16:19 — “And Moses said, Let no man leave of it till the morning” — the witnesses mark the rule of daily dependence.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: God would have them take no thought for the morrow but depend on him for daily bread — is not the petition in the Lord’s prayer founded on this? (Matthew 6:11) Ellicott: divinely instructed, that they might realise their absolute dependence on God day by day. The Reformed Calvin: God would supply their need afresh each day. Wesley: let them sleep quietly though not a bit of bread were in the camp, trusting God for the morrow.

The verse forbids hoarding. None is to keep the manna till morning. They must live by the day on God.

So the witnesses see the manna made a daily lesson in dependence: none was to be kept overnight. The witnesses note the command: “let no man leave of it till the morning” — each day’s portion was to be eaten that day, with nothing hoarded for the next; tomorrow’s supply must be sought afresh from God. They mark the purpose the expositors draw out: Ellicott says Moses was divinely instructed to issue this command, that Israel might realise their absolute dependence on God for food from day to day; Calvin notes that God would supply their need anew each morning, that they might continually look to him. They observe Clarke’s striking connection: God would have them take no thought for the morrow but trust him for their daily bread — and he asks whether the petition in the Lord’s prayer, “Give us this day our daily bread” (Matthew 6:11), is not founded on this very circumstance. They note Wesley’s vivid application: they were to learn to go to bed and sleep quietly though there were not a bit of bread in their tent or in all the camp, trusting God to bring it in the morning; never was a people so cast upon daily providence. They observe that the rule weaned them from the anxious storing-up that springs from distrust, and trained them to a daily faith. They note that the bread of heaven was given on terms of daily trust. They observe that this is the discipline of all who live by faith. The verse thus forbids hoarding: nothing kept till morning — a rule, the next verse shows, that some defied.
20:
On Exodus 16:20 — “Notwithstanding they hearkened not unto Moses; but some of them left of it until the morning, and it bred worms, and stank: and Moses was wroth with them” — the witnesses mark the disobedience and the corruption of the hoarded manna.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the punishment. Adam Clarke: their sinful curiosity and covetousness led them to try, and they had a mass of loathsome putrefaction for their pains; how gracious God, to render disobedience irksome. Ellicott: on the Sabbath it bred no worms (Exodus 16:24), so this was a punishment, not a natural result. The Reformed Gill: not all, but some, disobeyed.

The verse records the breach. Some kept the manna overnight; it bred worms and stank. Disobedience turned the gift to corruption.

So the witnesses see the first breaking of the manna rule swiftly and loathsomely punished. The witnesses note the disobedience: “they hearkened not unto Moses; but some of them left of it until the morning” — not all, Gill observes, but some, in distrust or covetousness, kept back a portion against the next day, contrary to the plain command. They mark the result: “it bred worms, and stank” — the hoarded manna rotted and crawled with worms, a mass of loathsome putrefaction. They observe Ellicott’s telling point that this was no natural decay, for the very same manna, kept over the Sabbath by God’s command, bred no worms and did not stink (Exodus 16:24); the corruption here was a judgment on the disobedience, God making the kept manna foul to rebuke the distrust that kept it. They note Clarke’s reflection on the mercy hidden in this severity: God rendered the sin irksome and unprofitable, so that their covetous laying-up brought them nothing but stench and worms; he makes disobedience its own punishment. They observe Moses’ anger: “Moses was wroth with them” — a righteous displeasure at their unbelief, so soon after the gift. They note the lesson: what is hoarded in distrust of God corrupts; the bread of heaven will not be turned to the uses of unbelief. They observe that God guards his own rule by the spoiling of what breaks it. The verse thus records the breach: the kept manna bred worms — while the daily gathering, the next verse shows, went on.
21:
On Exodus 16:21 — “And they gathered it every morning, every man according to his eating: and when the sun waxed hot, it melted” — the witnesses mark the daily gathering and the melting of the manna.

The Reformed Gill: they gathered it every morning, during the early hours, till about the fourth hour (ten o’clock) as the Targum says; a wonderful thing that bread should be provided afresh each day, and what was left on the ground melt with the sun’s heat.

The verse records the daily round. Each morning they gathered their portion; the sun’s heat melted the rest. The bread came and went with the day.

So the witnesses see the rhythm of the manna fixed to the morning, the leftover melting in the day’s heat. The witnesses note the daily diligence: “they gathered it every morning, every man according to his eating” — Gill observes, with the old Jewish reckoning, that they gathered in the early hours, until about the fourth hour of the day (ten o’clock); the manna was to be taken fresh at the day’s beginning, before the sun grew strong. They mark the wonder he draws out: it was a marvellous thing that bread should be thus provided anew every single morning for a whole nation, falling with the dew and lying ready to be gathered. They observe the melting: “when the sun waxed hot, it melted” — what was not gathered in the cool of the morning dissolved away under the heat of the day, so that the gathering could not be put off; the season for taking it was brief and must be seized. They note the discipline in this: the people were taught to rise early and seek their bread while it could be had, and not to presume on a later hour; the manna rewarded diligence and punished sloth. They observe the spiritual lesson the witnesses draw, that the bread of heaven, like Christ and his grace, is to be sought early and in season, not left till the heat of the day when the opportunity is gone. They note that the daily melting matched the daily falling, the gift renewed each dawn. The verse thus records the daily round: gathered each morning, melted by the sun — until the sixth day, the next verse shows, brought a double portion.
22:
On Exodus 16:22 — “And it came to pass, that on the sixth day they gathered twice as much bread… and all the rulers of the congregation came and told Moses” — the witnesses mark the double portion and the rulers’ surprise.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the sign. Adam Clarke: on the sixth day twice as much was gathered, for none fell on the Sabbath (Exodus 16:26) — a convincing miracle; no manna fell on the seventh day. Ellicott: the rulers were surprised and came for an explanation. The Reformed Calvin: the dense ignorance of the rulers, who had heard yet did not grasp the Sabbath provision.

The verse records the sixth-day gift. Twice as much falls; the rulers, surprised, come to Moses. The Sabbath provision is given.

So the witnesses see the sixth-day double portion fall as promised, to the surprise of the rulers. The witnesses note the miracle of measure: “on the sixth day they gathered twice as much bread, two omers for one man” — not that they gathered more by effort, but that twice the manna lay on the ground, a double supply against the coming Sabbath, on which (as the chapter shows) none would fall. They mark Clarke’s point that this was a convincing miracle: the manna, falling six days and never on the seventh, and falling double on the sixth, could be no mere natural product of the desert, but a thing ordered by God in honour of his Sabbath. They observe the reaction of the leaders: “all the rulers of the congregation came and told Moses” — Ellicott takes them to have been surprised at the unwonted abundance and to have come for an explanation. They note Calvin’s sharper reading, that this betrays the dense ignorance even of the rulers, who, though they had heard Moses foretell the sixth-day double portion (v. 5), had not laid it to heart and were taken aback when it came. They observe the patience of God, who, despite their dulness, still provided exactly as he had said, and gave Moses occasion to teach them the Sabbath. They note that the double gift both supplied the rest-day and proved the word already spoken. They observe that God’s provision honours God’s day. The verse thus records the sixth-day gift: a double portion, the rulers surprised — which Moses, in the next verse, explains by the Sabbath.
23:
On Exodus 16:23 — “This is that which the LORD hath said, To morrow is the rest of the holy sabbath unto the LORD: bake that which ye will bake to day… and that which remaineth over lay up… to be kept until the morning” — the witnesses mark the Sabbath rest and a question of its origin.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the rest. Keil and Delitzsch: tomorrow is the rest of the holy Sabbath; prepare today, lay up the rest. Adam Clarke and Ellicott: nothing here intimates the Sabbath was now first given; it is spoken of as already known. The Puritan Henry and Wesley: a plain intimation of the seventh-day Sabbath before Sinai, indeed from the beginning (Genesis 2:3).

The verse explains the double portion. Tomorrow is the holy Sabbath; prepare today and keep the rest. The day of rest is honoured.

So the witnesses see Moses explain the double portion by the coming Sabbath, and find in his words a witness to the Sabbath’s antiquity. The witnesses note the explanation: “to morrow is the rest of the holy sabbath unto the LORD” — the sixth day’s double gift was given because the seventh was a day of holy rest, on which they were not to gather; they should bake and seethe today what they needed, and lay up the remainder, which alone would keep. They mark the manner of the words, on which the expositors lay weight: Moses speaks of the Sabbath as a thing already known and established, not as a new institution; Clarke and Ellicott observe that nothing in the text intimates the Sabbath was now first given — it is named as “the holy sabbath,” familiar and understood. They observe the inference Henry and Wesley draw out at length: here is plain evidence of a seventh-day Sabbath kept before the giving of the law at Sinai, and indeed from the beginning, instituted at creation (Genesis 2:3); the manna did not create the Sabbath but honoured one already in being. They note the practical provision: the labour of preparing food was to be done on the sixth day, that the seventh might be wholly rest; God so arranged his gift as to make Sabbath-keeping possible. They observe that the day of rest is called “holy” and “unto the LORD,” set apart for him. They note that God taught the Sabbath by the very falling of the bread. The verse thus explains the double portion: the holy Sabbath honoured — the kept manna, the next verse shows, not corrupting on that day.
24:
On Exodus 16:24 — “And they laid it up till the morning, as Moses bade: and it did not stink, neither was there any worm therein” — the witnesses mark the Sabbath manna preserved.

The Reformed Gill: what was left of the two omers a man, which they had neither baked nor boiled, they laid up; and, against the nature of the manna on other days, it did not corrupt.

The verse records the preserving. The Sabbath portion, kept overnight, did not stink or breed worms. The miracle confirms the rest.

So the witnesses see the manna laid up for the Sabbath kept sweet and whole, in plain contrast to the hoarded manna of the weekdays. The witnesses note the obedience: “they laid it up till the morning, as Moses bade” — the remainder of the sixth day’s double portion, what they had not prepared, was kept overnight at Moses’ word, in trust of his promise that it would serve for the Sabbath; Gill notes it was the part they had neither baked nor boiled. They mark the wonder: “it did not stink, neither was there any worm therein” — the very manna that, kept on any other day, bred worms and stank (v. 20), now on the Sabbath remained sound and sweet. They observe the force of the contrast the witnesses draw: this proves the corruption of the weekday hoard was no natural process but a judgment, and the preservation here no natural keeping but a miracle; God made the same substance corrupt or keep according to his command, that the Sabbath might be honoured. They note that the standing weekly sign — double on the sixth, none on the seventh, the kept portion sound only over the Sabbath — set God’s seal upon the day of rest, working a continual miracle for its sake. They observe the reward of obedient trust: those who kept the manna at God’s bidding found it good, while those who kept it against his bidding found it foul. They note that God honours his own ordinance by a wonder. The verse thus records the preserving: the Sabbath manna kept whole — on which day, the next verse says, they were to eat it.
25:
On Exodus 16:25 — “And Moses said, Eat that to day; for to day is a sabbath unto the LORD: to day ye shall not find it in the field” — the witnesses mark the Sabbath eating and the manna’s absence.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: “to day is a sabbath” — a rest; by these words the Sabbath was instituted or re-instituted, its character of a weekly rest assigned, marked by its name. The Reformed Gill: Moses bids them eat the kept manna, baked or boiled or as it was; they had no other, none falling that day.

The verse directs the Sabbath meal. Eat today the kept manna, for none lies in the field. The rest-day is provided for and proved.

So the witnesses see Moses direct the people to eat, on the Sabbath, the manna laid up the day before, since none was to be found in the field. The witnesses note the command: “eat that to day; for to day is a sabbath unto the LORD” — the portion kept from the sixth day was to be their food on the seventh, whether baked, boiled, or eaten as it was; Gill observes they had no other, for none fell that day. They mark the reason given: “to day ye shall not find it in the field” — the manna, which lay every other morning, did not appear on the Sabbath; the people need not, and indeed could not, gather it, the day being one of rest. They observe Ellicott’s comment that in these words the Sabbath is set forth (instituted or re-instituted to Israel) with its essential character of a weekly rest, marked even by its name, “a rest unto the LORD.” They note the double provision and proof: God both supplied the rest-day beforehand by the double portion and proved its holiness by withholding the manna upon it; the empty field on the seventh morning was itself a sermon on the Sabbath. They observe that the people were thus freed from labour on the day, having their bread already in hand. They note that God’s ordering of the bread taught them to keep his day. The verse thus directs the Sabbath meal: eat the kept manna, for none lies in the field — the rest enjoined again, against the gatherers, in the next verses.
26:
On Exodus 16:26 — “Six days ye shall gather it; but on the seventh day, which is the sabbath, in it there shall be none” — the witnesses mark the weekly rule of the manna.

The Reformed Gill: six days, day after day, every morning, they should gather it, as they had done, during all their stay in the wilderness; but on the seventh, the Sabbath, none would fall to be gathered.

The verse fixes the weekly rule. Six days the manna falls and is gathered; on the seventh, none. The pattern of work and rest is set.

So the witnesses see the manna’s rhythm laid down as a fixed weekly rule for all their wilderness years. The witnesses note the command: “six days ye shall gather it” — Gill observes that day after day, every morning, through all their long sojourn in the wilderness, they were to go out and gather their portion, as they had begun. They mark the exception: “on the seventh day, which is the sabbath, in it there shall be none” — on the Sabbath no manna would fall, so there was nothing to gather; the rest-day was marked by the very absence of the bread. They observe the steadiness of the arrangement: this was no passing provision but a settled order, the manna falling six days and never the seventh for the whole forty years, a continual testimony to the Sabbath written into the people’s daily bread. They note the pattern the witnesses see in it: six days of labour and gathering, one day of rest, mirroring the order of creation and the commandment soon to be given at Sinai; the week of work and rest was thus impressed on Israel before the law was spoken. They observe that God so framed his gift as to teach and enforce the keeping of his day, by giving abundantly before it and nothing upon it. They note that the rest was built into the very supply of their food. They observe the mercy and the discipline joined: bread for six days, rest on the seventh. The verse thus fixes the weekly rule: six days to gather, none on the Sabbath — which some, the next verse shows, would not believe.
27:
On Exodus 16:27 — “And it came to pass, that there went out some of the people on the seventh day for to gather, and they found none” — the witnesses mark the unbelief of the Sabbath gatherers.

The Reformed Calvin: this is the second transgression — by going out on the seventh day they trenched upon its observance; their monstrous greediness arose from not believing what Moses had plainly declared, that none would fall.

The verse records the second breach. Some go out on the Sabbath to gather, and find none. Their unbelief is rebuked by the empty ground.

So the witnesses see a second breaking of God’s rule about the manna: some go out on the Sabbath, against the command, and find nothing. The witnesses note the act: “there went out some of the people on the seventh day for to gather” — not all, but some, in defiance or distrust of the word Moses had given, went out to seek manna on the day they had been told none would fall. They mark Calvin’s judgment that this was the second transgression in the matter of the manna (the first being the hoarding of v. 20): by going out on the seventh day they trespassed upon the holy rest, and so dishonoured the Sabbath. They observe his reading of the root of it: their “monstrous greediness” sprang from unbelief, from their not crediting what Moses had so plainly declared, that on the Sabbath there would be none; they would not take God at his word, but must go and see. They note the result: “they found none” — the bare ground rebuked their unbelief; God made good his word both in the giving on six days and in the withholding on the seventh, so that their fruitless search exposed their folly. They observe the lesson the witnesses draw: distrust of God’s plain word leads to needless labour and to the breaking of his commands; faith would have rested, but unbelief went out and was shamed. They note that the empty field preached the Sabbath as loudly as the falling manna. They observe that God vindicated his rest by their failure. The verse thus records the second breach: the Sabbath gatherers found none — which draws, in the next verse, God’s rebuke.
28:
On Exodus 16:28 — “And the LORD said unto Moses, How long refuse ye to keep my commandments and my laws?” — the witnesses mark God’s rebuke of persistent disobedience.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: how long refuse ye to keep my commandments? Charles Ellicott: the people had already broken one precept about the manna (v. 20); now they broke another, at least in spirit, in seeking what was not there. The Reformed Gill: the Lord, who had seen the Sabbath gatherers, was displeased and spoke to Moses out of the cloud.

The verse gives God’s reproof. How long, he asks, will they refuse his commandments? The repeated breaches provoke his rebuke.

So the witnesses see God himself rebuke the people’s repeated refusal to keep his word about the manna and the Sabbath. The witnesses note the searching question: “How long refuse ye to keep my commandments and my laws?” — a reproof addressed through Moses to the whole people, though prompted by the Sabbath gatherers; Gill notes that the Lord, who had seen them go out, was displeased and spoke out of the cloud. They mark the force of “how long”: this was not a first or isolated fault but one of a string of disobediences. Ellicott observes that the people had already broken one positive precept concerning the manna, in keeping it overnight (v. 20), and now broke another, at least in spirit, by going out to gather on the Sabbath; the rebuke takes in their settled bent to transgress. They observe the weight God lays on these seemingly small commands: the keeping or breaking of his rule about the manna and the rest-day was, in his eyes, the keeping or refusing of “my commandments and my laws”; obedience in little things is obedience to God. They note that the question is grieved as well as stern, the “how long” of a patience tried by repeated unbelief. They observe the mercy that God reproves rather than at once destroys, seeking to bring them to obedience. They note that the rebuke prepares for the renewed charge of the next verse. The verse thus gives God’s reproof: how long will ye refuse my commandments? — answered by the fresh command to keep the rest.
29:
On Exodus 16:29 — “See, for that the LORD hath given you the sabbath, therefore he giveth you on the sixth day the bread of two days; abide ye every man in his place… on the seventh day” — the witnesses mark the Sabbath a gift, and the charge to abide.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: “abide ye every man in his place” — neither go out for manna nor for any other purpose; rest at home, given to religious exercises. Ellicott: some Jews took this absolutely literally, staying in the very posture they woke in, but this slavish reading was generally repudiated. The Reformed Gill: the words of the LORD, or of Moses from him.

The verse names the Sabbath a gift. Because God gave the rest, he gives the sixth-day double bread; let each abide at home. The day is a mercy, not a burden.

So the witnesses see God set the Sabbath before the people as a gift, and ground the double portion in it. The witnesses note the gracious framing: “the LORD hath given you the sabbath” — the rest-day is named not as a burden laid on them but as a gift bestowed; and “therefore he giveth you on the sixth day the bread of two days” — the double portion is itself a token of the gift, God providing beforehand so that they may rest without want. They mark the charge: “abide ye every man in his place… let no man go out of his place on the seventh day” — on the Sabbath they were to stay at home, not roaming out to gather manna (which was not there) nor about other common business. They observe Clarke’s sense that the rest was to be filled with religious exercises, the time freed from labour given to God. They note the caution Ellicott records about over-literal readings: some Jews took “abide in his place” so strictly as to remain all day in the very posture in which they woke, but this slavish letter was generally and rightly rejected; the command forbids the labour of going out for food and gain, not all movement whatever. They observe the tenderness of the verse: God presents his day as a kindness, having so ordered the manna that resting costs them nothing. They note that the Sabbath is a mercy to man, a gift of rest from a bountiful God. The verse thus names the Sabbath a gift and charges them to abide — whereupon, the next verse says, the people rested.
30:
On Exodus 16:30 — “So the people rested on the seventh day” — the witnesses mark the people’s rest.

The Reformed Gill: they did not attempt to go out of their tents in quest of manna, as on other days, but observed it as a day of rest from labour; and so they continued to do in succeeding generations.

The verse records obedience. The people rested on the seventh day. The Sabbath is kept at last.

So the witnesses see the people, after the rebuke, settle into the keeping of the Sabbath rest. The witnesses note the plain obedience: “so the people rested on the seventh day” — Gill observes that they did not, as some had done, go out of their tents in quest of manna as on the working days, but kept the day as one of rest from labour. They mark the turn from the foregoing disobedience: where some had hoarded the manna and others gone out to gather on the Sabbath, now the people as a whole submit to the command and rest. They observe the lasting effect Gill draws out: this was not merely a single Sabbath kept, but the beginning of a continued observance, the people resting on the seventh day “in succeeding generations”; the wilderness teaching of the manna fixed the Sabbath in Israel’s life before ever the law thundered it from Sinai. They note the fruit of God’s patient dealing: by the double portion, the withholding, the spoiling of the hoard, and the rebuke, he brought a murmuring and disobedient people to honour his rest. They observe the quietness of the verse, a brief record of obedience after much resistance: the people simply rested. They note that the Sabbath, given as a gift, is here received as a duty kept. They observe that God’s ordinance prevailed. The verse thus records the obedience: the people rested on the seventh day — the chapter turning, in the next verse, to name and describe the manna.

So the witnesses see the chapter reach its quiet resolution in the obedience of a people long resistant. They note that the rest kept here was the fruit of God’s patient and varied dealing: the double portion on the sixth day, the withholding on the seventh, the breeding of worms in the hoarded manna, the empty field that shamed the Sabbath gatherers, and the searching rebuke — all together brought the murmuring congregation to honour the rest God had given. They observe that the rest is named simply, without flourish: after so much teaching, “the people rested,” the bare record of obedience at last yielded. They mark that this rest, Gill notes, was no isolated act but the seed of a settled custom carried into the generations that followed, so that the manna in the wilderness schooled Israel in Sabbath-keeping before the law was spoken at Sinai. They observe that what God gives as a gift he also requires as a duty, and that his people enter his rest not by their own readiness but by his persevering grace overcoming their unbelief. They note that the Sabbath, so honoured by God in the very falling of the bread, was now honoured by the people in the ceasing of their toil.
31:
On Exodus 16:31 — “And the house of Israel called the name thereof Manna: and it was like coriander seed, white; and the taste of it was like wafers made with honey” — the witnesses mark the name, look, and taste of the manna.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the description. Charles Ellicott: rather “man”; “manna” is a Greek form from the LXX; like coriander seed, compared above to hoar frost. The Reformed Gill: till now they had given it no name. Calvin: Moses repeats the name, that they might be condemned who dared despise so evident a miracle.

The verse names and describes it. Israel called it Manna; white like coriander seed, tasting of honeyed wafers. The bread of heaven is set before us.

So the witnesses see the manna at last given its name and described in its look and taste. The witnesses note the naming: “the house of Israel called the name thereof Manna” — Gill observes that until now they had given it no settled name, the word having arisen from their first question, “What is this?” (v. 15); Ellicott notes that the form “manna” is a Greek shaping of the Hebrew man, first used by the Septuagint. They mark the appearance: “like coriander seed, white” — small round white grains, as before it was likened to the hoar frost; a fine, pale, seed-like substance. They observe the taste: “like wafers made with honey” — sweet and pleasant, as thin cakes sweetened with honey (elsewhere likened to fresh oil, Numbers 11:8); the bread of heaven was delightful to the taste. They note Calvin’s point that Moses dwells on the description and repeats the name to leave without excuse any who should dare to despise so evident and well-attested a miracle; the manna was no vague tradition but a thing seen, named, handled, and tasted by a whole nation. They observe the typology the witnesses carry through: this sweet white bread from heaven is a figure of Christ, the true Manna, the Bread of Life, sweet to those who feed on him (John 6:35; Psalm 78:24). They note that God’s gift was both nourishing and pleasant. The verse thus names and describes it: Manna, white, honey-sweet — a portion of which, the next verses command, was to be kept.
32:
On Exodus 16:32 — “This is the thing which the LORD commandeth, Fill an omer of it to be kept for your generations; that they may see the bread wherewith I have fed you in the wilderness” — the witnesses mark the manna kept for a memorial.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the command. The Puritan Henry: an omer of it was laid up in a golden pot (Hebrews 9:4), kept for a memorial. The Reformed Calvin: Moses interposes this by anticipation, confirming that the food was created by God’s special bounty, since he desired an omer preserved.

The verse commands a memorial. An omer of manna is to be kept for the generations, that they may see the wilderness bread. The mercy is to be remembered.

So the witnesses see God command that a portion of the manna be preserved as a lasting memorial of his provision. The witnesses note the charge: “fill an omer of it to be kept for your generations” — a single omer, the daily portion of one man, was to be laid up and preserved, not eaten, that it might remain. They mark the purpose: “that they may see the bread wherewith I have fed you in the wilderness” — the kept manna was to be a witness to later generations, who had not tasted it, of how God had fed their fathers in the desert when there was no bread; the eye was to confirm the report. They observe Henry’s note, drawn from the Epistle to the Hebrews, that this omer was laid up in a golden pot and kept in the ark (Hebrews 9:4), among the holiest memorials of God’s dealings. They note Calvin’s observation that Moses sets this command here by anticipation (the keeping belonging to a later time, when the tabernacle was built), the more to confirm that the manna was a special creation of God’s bounty — for why preserve a sample of common food? They observe the wonder that the preserved manna did not corrupt as the hoarded manna did, a standing miracle in the sanctuary. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: God would have his mercies remembered, kept before the eyes of after-generations, that his faithfulness be not forgotten. They observe that the manna in the pot preached to those who never gathered it. The verse thus commands a memorial: an omer kept for the generations — which Moses, in the next verse, bids Aaron lay up.
33:
On Exodus 16:33 — “And Moses said unto Aaron, Take a pot, and put an omer full of manna therein, and lay it up before the LORD, to be kept for your generations” — the witnesses mark the manna laid up before the LORD.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: an omer full of manna put in a pot and laid up before the LORD. Charles Ellicott: laid up before the Testimony, the two tables; the writer to the Hebrews names the golden pot among the ark’s contents (Hebrews 9:4). The Reformed Gill: the Targum and Jarchi call it an earthen pot — an emblem, perhaps, of the ministers who bear the gospel treasure in earthen vessels.

The verse executes the command. Aaron is to put an omer of manna in a pot and lay it before the LORD. The memorial is placed in the sanctuary.

So the witnesses see Moses direct Aaron to carry out the keeping of the memorial manna. The witnesses note the charge: “take a pot, and put an omer full of manna therein” — a vessel was to be filled with the appointed omer and set apart. They mark a small difference the expositors note about the pot: the Epistle to the Hebrews, following the Septuagint, calls it a “golden pot” (Hebrews 9:4), while the Targum and Jarchi, Gill observes, speak of an earthen one; Gill draws from the latter a fancy that an earthen vessel would aptly emblem the ministers of the word, who bear the gospel treasure in earthen vessels. They observe the place of keeping: “lay it up before the LORD” — before the divine presence, and (as the next verse and Hebrews show) before the Testimony, in or by the ark of the covenant, among the sacred memorials. They note that the manna was thus given a place in the very heart of the sanctuary, beside the tables of the law and Aaron’s rod, a perpetual witness to God’s feeding of his people. They observe that the command, given to Aaron the priest, makes the keeping of the memorial a priestly and holy charge, not a common one. They note the fitness of laying the bread of heaven before the LORD who gave it. They observe that the gift is enshrined where God dwells. The verse thus executes the command: the manna put in a pot, laid up before the LORD — which Aaron, in the next verse, does as commanded.
34:
On Exodus 16:34 — “As the LORD commanded Moses, so Aaron laid it up before the Testimony, to be kept” — the witnesses mark the obedient keeping of the manna.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: the “testimony” belonged to the tabernacle, not yet built; the keeping was done when the ark was made. The Reformed Gill: before the ark of the testimony, so called because it contained the law. Wesley: kept before the testimony; its preservation from waste and corruption was a standing miracle, the more proper for a memorial.

The verse records the obedience. Aaron lays the manna up before the Testimony, as commanded. The memorial is faithfully kept.

So the witnesses see Aaron faithfully execute the charge, laying up the manna before the Testimony. The witnesses note the obedience: “as the LORD commanded Moses, so Aaron laid it up” — the keeping was done exactly as directed, the priest carrying out the divine command without alteration. They mark the place: “before the Testimony” — Gill explains this as before the ark of the testimony, so called because it held the tables of the law, the “testimony” of God’s covenant. They observe the note of time the expositors add: Clarke and others remark that the Testimony and the ark belonged to the tabernacle, which was not yet built when the manna first fell, so that the actual laying-up was accomplished later, when the sanctuary was reared; the verse records by anticipation what was then done. They note Wesley’s striking observation that the very preservation of this manna — kept whole and uncorrupted for generations, when the daily manna would not last a single night — was itself a standing, continual miracle, which made it all the fitter to be a memorial of God’s power and bounty. They observe that the kept manna thus testified twice over: to the past feeding in the wilderness, and, by its own incorruption, to the ongoing power of God. They note the faithfulness of Aaron in a sacred trust. They observe that the memorial was secured for the generations to come. The verse thus records the obedience: Aaron laid the manna before the Testimony — and the chapter closes, in the next verses, with the span of the manna and the measure of the omer.
35:
On Exodus 16:35 — “And the children of Israel did eat manna forty years, until they came to a land inhabited; they did eat manna, until they came unto the borders of the land of Canaan” — the witnesses mark the forty years of manna.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the span. Adam Clarke: from this it has been supposed the book was written after the manna ceased; but the words might be added by Ezra, or by Moses near his death. Charles Ellicott: Moses may have added this shortly before his death, when the manna had continued thirty-nine years and nine months. The Reformed Gill: forty years, wanting thirty days, from the Exodus to their coming to the borders of Canaan at Gilgal.

The verse records the duration. Israel ate manna forty years, until they reached the borders of Canaan. God’s provision lasted the whole journey.

So the witnesses see the manna continue, not for a season only, but for the whole forty years of the wilderness wandering. The witnesses note the long faithfulness: “the children of Israel did eat manna forty years, until they came to a land inhabited” — the heavenly bread that began in the wilderness of Sin did not fail until they reached the borders of Canaan, a settled and fruitful land; Gill reckons it forty years all but thirty days, from the Exodus to their coming to Gilgal. They mark the constancy of the gift: day by day, six days a week, for nearly four decades, a whole nation was fed from heaven; the wonder lay not only in the manna’s beginning but in its long unbroken continuance. They observe the critical question the expositors raise about the verse’s authorship: Clarke notes that the words, looking back on the manna as a thing accomplished, have led some to suppose the book was written after the manna ceased; he and Ellicott answer that the verse may have been added by Moses himself near the end of his life (when the manna had run some thirty-nine years and nine months), or inserted later by Ezra under inspiration, without impugning Mosaic authorship of the whole. They observe the faithfulness of God the verse declares: he who began to feed them kept feeding them all the way, to the very edge of the promised rest. They note that the manna ceased only when the land’s own bread began (Joshua 5:12). The verse thus records the duration: manna for forty years, to the borders of Canaan — the omer’s measure being explained in the closing verse.
36:
On Exodus 16:36 — “Now an omer is the tenth part of an ephah” — the witnesses mark the explanatory note on the measure.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: an omer about six pints English; the verse’s true place seems after Exodus 16:18, for here it has no connection. Charles Ellicott: the omer and the ephah were Egyptian measures; the ephah continued in use till the captivity, the omer fell out early, hence this parenthetic explanation. The Reformed Gill notes the account given of the measure.

The verse explains the omer. An omer is a tenth of an ephah. The daily measure is defined for later readers.

So the witnesses see the chapter close with a brief note defining the omer, the measure so often named in the story of the manna. The witnesses note the explanation: “an omer is the tenth part of an ephah” — the omer, the daily portion of manna for each person, is fixed in relation to the better-known ephah, of which it was a tenth; Clarke reckons it at about six English pints. They mark the reason the expositors give for so technical a note: Ellicott observes that both the omer and the ephah were originally Egyptian measures, but that while the ephah remained in common use among the Hebrews down to the captivity, the omer fell out of use very early; hence this parenthetic explanation was added, that later readers, to whom the omer was unfamiliar, might understand how much each man’s daily portion had been. They observe Clarke’s further remark that the verse seems out of its natural place, reading as if it belonged rather after the account of the gathering (Exodus 16:18), where the omer is first measured, than at the very end of the chapter. They note the care of Scripture, or of its later editor, to make the record plain to every generation, explaining an obsolete term. They observe that the small note serves the larger purpose of the chapter: that the wonder of the manna, down to the very measure of it, be clearly known and remembered. They note that nothing of God’s provision is too small to record. The verse thus explains the omer: a tenth of an ephah — closing the chapter of the manna.
